items.subtopic: Health Recommendations for International Travel
items.title: Impact of Modern Air Travel on Infectious Disease Spread
items.content: The rapid pace of modern air travel significantly contributes to the global spread of infectious diseases. The reduced travel time, now less than 24 hours globally, allows travelers to reach destinations before symptom onset, facilitating the transmission of infections.
keyPoints: International tourist arrivals increased by 50.3% from 2010 to 2019.
Air travel duration is shorter than incubation periods for most infections.
Examples of epidemics linked to air travel include SARS in 2003, Ebola in 2014, and COVID-19 in 2020.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · Health Recommendations for International Travel

items.subtopic: Health Recommendations for International Travel
items.title: Diverse Risks Associated with Modern International Travelers
items.content: The diversity of international travelers has increased, encompassing various demographics such as students, migrants, and medical tourists. This includes individuals at higher risk for illness or injury, like the elderly, infants, pregnant women, and those with chronic conditions.
keyPoints: Types of modern travelers include students, migrants, medical tourists, and VFR travelers.
Increased travel by high-risk groups: elderly, infants, pregnant women, immunocompromised individuals.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · Health Recommendations for International Travel

items.subtopic: EPIDEMIOLOGY OF TRAVEL-RELATED CONDITIONS
items.title: High Incidence of Travel-Related Illnesses
items.content: A significant proportion of travelers experience travel-related illnesses, with diarrhea being the most common. Despite this high incidence, hospitalization rates remain low.
keyPoints: 43–79% of travelers report developing a travel-related illness.
<1–3% of travelers report hospitalization.
Diarrhea is the most commonly reported travel-related illness.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · EPIDEMIOLOGY OF TRAVEL-RELATED CONDITIONS

items.subtopic: EPIDEMIOLOGY OF TRAVEL-RELATED CONDITIONS
items.title: Common Causes of Death Among Travelers
items.content: Cardiovascular events and injuries are more common causes of death among travelers than infections, with motor vehicle accidents being the leading cause of injury-related deaths.
keyPoints: Cardiovascular events and injuries are more common causes of death than infections.
Motor vehicle accidents are the most common cause of injury-related deaths among U.S. citizens traveling internationally.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · EPIDEMIOLOGY OF TRAVEL-RELATED CONDITIONS

items.subtopic: GENERAL APPROACH TO ADVISING INTERNATIONAL TRAVELERS
items.title: Importance of Detailed Itinerary in Trip Risk Assessment
items.content: A comprehensive itinerary is essential for assessing travel risks and determining specific preventive measures, including vaccinations and malaria prophylaxis.
keyPoints: Detailed itinerary includes cities, activities, accommodations, trip duration, sequence of countries, and transit stops.
Assessment helps determine indications for specific vaccines like yellow fever.
Recommendations vary significantly among countries; CDC website offers up-to-date guidance.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · GENERAL APPROACH TO ADVISING INTERNATIONAL TRAVELERS

items.subtopic: GENERAL APPROACH TO ADVISING INTERNATIONAL TRAVELERS
items.title: Consideration of Traveler's Medical Profile in Preventive Measures
items.content: The traveler's medical profile, including age, medical history, current medications, allergies, and pregnancy status, is crucial for tailoring preventive measures.
keyPoints: Key considerations include immunocompromising conditions, cardiac and pulmonary issues, pregnancy, and severe allergies.
Hepatic and renal impairment can influence malaria prophylaxis choices.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · GENERAL APPROACH TO ADVISING INTERNATIONAL TRAVELERS

items.subtopic: GENERAL APPROACH TO ADVISING INTERNATIONAL TRAVELERS
items.title: Timing and Referral for Pretravel Consultations
items.content: Referrals to travel clinics should ideally occur 4–6 weeks before travel, but consultations can still be beneficial even if this timeframe is not met.
keyPoints: Vaccines take a week or more to induce immunity; early consultation is ideal.
Complex itineraries or medical histories may necessitate referral to specialists.
Primary care providers should routinely inquire about travel plans.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · GENERAL APPROACH TO ADVISING INTERNATIONAL TRAVELERS

items.subtopic: IMMUNIZATIONS FOR TRAVELERS
items.title: Understanding Travel Immunization Categories and Requirements
items.content: Travelers must be aware of the different categories of immunizations: routine, recommended, and required. Routine vaccines are essential for all travelers to prevent infections encountered worldwide. Recommended vaccines address specific risks associated with travel destinations not covered by routine schedules. Required vaccinations are mandated by international regulations or specific countries for entry or exit, often documented on an International Certificate of Vaccination Prophylaxis (ICVP).
keyPoints: Routine immunizations should be up to date for all travelers.
Recommended vaccines address risks not covered in routine schedules.
Required immunizations must be documented on the ICVP and may involve medical waivers.
Vaccines like yellow fever might be required even if not routinely recommended for a specific country.
Live virus vaccines should be given on the same day or spaced at least 28 days apart to ensure proper immunogenicity.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · IMMUNIZATIONS FOR TRAVELERS

items.subtopic: IMMUNIZATIONS FOR TRAVELERS
items.title: Scheduling and Administration of Travel Vaccines
items.content: Travel vaccines can generally be administered on the same day, with exceptions such as oral typhoid vaccine, which should be given at least 8 hours after oral cholera vaccine. Live virus vaccines (e.g., yellow fever, MMR) require careful scheduling to maintain immunogenicity; they should either be administered simultaneously or spaced by at least 28 days. If neither schedule is feasible, the second vaccination must be repeated after at least 28 days.
keyPoints: Oral typhoid vaccine should be given at least 8 hours after oral cholera vaccine.
Live virus vaccines should be administered on the same day or spaced at least 28 days apart.
If live virus vaccines are not scheduled appropriately, repeat the second dose after at least 28 days.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · IMMUNIZATIONS FOR TRAVELERS

items.subtopic: IMMUNIZATIONS FOR TRAVELERS TO MOST DESTINATIONS (part 1)
items.title: Hepatitis A Immunization for Travelers
items.content: Travelers to regions with inadequate sanitation, particularly South Asia and sub-Saharan Africa, are at high risk of hepatitis A. Vaccination is recommended prior to travel, with a single dose providing protection for younger, healthy adults. For those over 40 or immunocompromised, consider adding immune globulin.
keyPoints: Transmission occurs primarily through fecal-oral route and contaminated food/water.
High risk in countries with poor sanitation; endemicity highest in South Asia and sub-Saharan Africa.
Single dose of monovalent hepatitis A vaccine is protective for younger, healthy adults when given before travel.
Booster dose 6–18 months after the primary dose confers lifelong immunity.
For persons >40 years old or immunocompromised, consider administering hepatitis A immune globulin (0.1 mL/kg) at a separate site with primary vaccination.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · IMMUNIZATIONS FOR TRAVELERS TO MOST DESTINATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: IMMUNIZATIONS FOR TRAVELERS TO MOST DESTINATIONS (part 1)
items.title: Hepatitis B Vaccination for Travelers
items.content: Travelers should avoid high-risk activities like tattooing and unprotected sex to prevent hepatitis B. For those unable to complete the standard vaccine schedule, consider rapid combined vaccines or novel adjuvant options.
keyPoints: Transmission through contact with contaminated blood or bodily fluids.
Avoid high-risk activities such as tattooing, body piercing, and unprotected sexual intercourse.
Nonimmune travelers departing soon can use Twinrix series or Heplisav-B for rapid protection.
Particularly important for long-term travelers, healthcare workers, and those having sexual encounters.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · IMMUNIZATIONS FOR TRAVELERS TO MOST DESTINATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: IMMUNIZATIONS FOR TRAVELERS TO MOST DESTINATIONS (part 1)
items.title: Influenza and COVID-19 Vaccinations for Travelers
items.content: Travelers should be up to date with influenza and COVID-19 vaccines, even if traveling during peak local seasons. Consider additional respiratory infection vaccines for high-risk individuals.
keyPoints: Respiratory infections are the most common vaccine-preventable diseases acquired during travel.
Influenza vaccine recommended year-round for unvaccinated travelers, especially in tropical regions.
Emphasize importance of vaccinations to prevent disruption and protect contacts.
Consider additional vaccines like pneumococcus and RSV for those at higher risk of severe illness.
Preventative measures include wearing masks and avoiding crowded areas.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · IMMUNIZATIONS FOR TRAVELERS TO MOST DESTINATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: IMMUNIZATIONS FOR TRAVELERS TO MOST DESTINATIONS (part 2)
items.title: Measles Immunization for Travelers
items.content: Travelers should ensure immunity to measles due to global resurgence and transmission risks, especially in airports and on aircraft. The criteria for U.S. adults include documented vaccination with two doses of MMR vaccine at least 28 days apart, serologic evidence of immunity, laboratory-confirmed illness, or birth before 1957.
keyPoints: Global increase in measles cases since 1996
Criteria for immunity: two doses of MMR vaccine, serologic evidence, lab-confirmed illness, or birth before 1957
Risk due to outbreaks initiated by unvaccinated travelers
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · IMMUNIZATIONS FOR TRAVELERS TO MOST DESTINATIONS (part 2)

items.subtopic: IMMUNIZATIONS FOR TRAVELERS TO MOST DESTINATIONS (part 2)
items.title: Tetanus, Diphtheria, and Pertussis Vaccination Recommendations
items.content: Travelers should be up to date with tetanus, diphtheria, and pertussis vaccinations. A tetanus booster is recommended for injuries if the last dose was over 5 years ago, especially in high-risk areas. A diphtheria booster within 5 years is advised for travelers to outbreak regions.
keyPoints: Tetanus booster recommended if last dose >5 years before injury
Diphtheria booster recommended within 5 years of travel to outbreak areas
Pertussis risk exists worldwide
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · IMMUNIZATIONS FOR TRAVELERS TO MOST DESTINATIONS (part 2)

items.subtopic: IMMUNIZATIONS FOR TRAVELERS TO MOST DESTINATIONS (part 2)
items.title: Typhoid Fever Vaccination for Travelers
items.content: Travelers to high-risk areas like South Asia, Africa, and Southeast Asia should consider typhoid vaccination due to transmission through contaminated food and water. Two vaccines are available: injectable Vi capsular polysaccharide vaccine and oral live attenuated vaccine (contraindicated for immunocompromised). Neither protects against paratyphoid fever.
keyPoints: High-risk areas: South Asia, Africa, Southeast Asia
Vaccines: Injectable Vi capsular polysaccharide or oral live attenuated (not for immunocompromised)
No protection against paratyphoid fever
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · IMMUNIZATIONS FOR TRAVELERS TO MOST DESTINATIONS (part 2)

items.subtopic: IMMUNIZATIONS FOR CERTAIN REGIONS OR SITUATIONS (part 1)
items.title: Yellow Fever Vaccination Requirements and Validity
items.content: Travelers to endemic regions of yellow fever must often provide proof of immunization, which is valid for entry purposes starting 10 days post-vaccination. The WHO no longer requires a booster every 10 years, although it's recommended for specific high-risk groups.
keyPoints: Yellow fever vaccine is valid for entry beginning 10 days after administration and extends for the lifetime of the vaccine.
A single dose provides long-term protection; WHO removed the 10-year boost requirement in 2016.
Booster doses recommended for certain individuals, such as those with severe immunosuppression or high-risk travel.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · IMMUNIZATIONS FOR CERTAIN REGIONS OR SITUATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: IMMUNIZATIONS FOR CERTAIN REGIONS OR SITUATIONS (part 1)
items.title: Contraindications and Precautions for Yellow Fever Vaccine
items.content: The yellow fever vaccine is contraindicated in individuals with severe immunosuppression, malignancies, thymus gland disorders, or severe egg allergies. Certain precautions apply to adults over 60, pregnant women, breastfeeding mothers, and those with asymptomatic HIV infection.
keyPoints: Contraindications include severe immunosuppression (e.g., CD4+ T lymphocyte count of <200/μL), malignancies, thymus gland disorders, and severe egg allergies.
Precautions for adults: age ≥60 years, pregnancy, breastfeeding, asymptomatic HIV with a CD4+ count of 200–499/μL.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · IMMUNIZATIONS FOR CERTAIN REGIONS OR SITUATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: IMMUNIZATIONS FOR CERTAIN REGIONS OR SITUATIONS (part 1)
items.title: Poliomyelitis Vaccination Recommendations for Travelers
items.content: Adults with a complete childhood polio vaccination series should receive an inactivated poliovirus vaccine booster when traveling to areas with recent wild-type or circulating vaccine-derived poliovirus transmission. Exit requirements may apply for stays over 4 weeks in high-risk countries.
keyPoints: Booster recommended for adults with primary childhood vaccination, traveling to regions with recent poliovirus transmission.
Exit requirements for proof of vaccination can apply for stays >4 weeks in certain high-risk countries.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · IMMUNIZATIONS FOR CERTAIN REGIONS OR SITUATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: IMMUNIZATIONS FOR CERTAIN REGIONS OR SITUATIONS (part 2)
items.title: Cholera Vaccination Considerations for Travelers
items.content: For travelers to cholera-endemic regions, particularly those experiencing outbreaks or unable to maintain strict hygienic practices, vaccination is advised. The single-dose live attenuated oral vaccine Vaxchora shows high efficacy shortly after administration.
keyPoints: Cholera risk is low with safe food and water precautions.
Vaccination recommended for travelers to endemic regions or those unable to adhere to hygienic practices.
High-risk individuals include those with type O blood or comorbid conditions, and in areas with limited healthcare access.
Vaxchora efficacy: 90% at 10 days, 80% at 3 months post-vaccination.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · IMMUNIZATIONS FOR CERTAIN REGIONS OR SITUATIONS (part 2)

items.subtopic: IMMUNIZATIONS FOR CERTAIN REGIONS OR SITUATIONS (part 2)
items.title: Meningococcal Disease Vaccination for High-Risk Travelers
items.content: Travelers to the African meningitis belt during the dry season or pilgrims to Saudi Arabia should receive quadrivalent meningococcal vaccine. Conjugated vaccines are preferred over polysaccharide due to better immunogenicity.
keyPoints: High-risk areas include the African 'meningitis belt' during December to June.
Quadrivalent vaccine protects against serogroups A, C, Y, and W-135.
Conjugated vaccines are preferred; polysaccharide vaccines no longer available in the U.S.
Pilgrims to Saudi Arabia must show proof of vaccination within 3 years.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · IMMUNIZATIONS FOR CERTAIN REGIONS OR SITUATIONS (part 2)

items.subtopic: IMMUNIZATIONS FOR CERTAIN REGIONS OR SITUATIONS (part 3)
items.title: Rabies Preexposure and Postexposure Prophylaxis
items.content: Travelers at higher risk for rabies exposure, such as those visiting areas without routine pet vaccination or engaging in high-risk activities, should consider preexposure immunization. The updated CDC regimen involves a two-dose series administered 7 days apart. For postexposure prophylaxis, rabies immune globulin and a vaccination series are required.
keyPoints: Rabies is endemic worldwide except Antarctica; dogs are common sources in regions without animal control.
Preexposure immunization involves a two-dose series (7 days apart) revised in 2022 for high-risk travelers.
Postexposure prophylaxis requires rabies immune globulin and vaccination series.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · IMMUNIZATIONS FOR CERTAIN REGIONS OR SITUATIONS (part 3)

items.subtopic: IMMUNIZATIONS FOR CERTAIN REGIONS OR SITUATIONS (part 3)
items.title: Tick-Borne Encephalitis Vaccination Strategy
items.content: Travelers to endemic regions in Europe and Asia should consider vaccination against tick-borne encephalitis, especially if engaging in outdoor activities. The vaccine is administered as a three-dose series, but two doses may suffice for short-term travelers.
keyPoints: Tick-borne encephalitis is transmitted by Ixodes ticks and through unpasteurized dairy products.
Vaccination involves a three-dose primary series; two doses are recommended for short-term travelers.
Personal protective measures against tick bites are advised.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · IMMUNIZATIONS FOR CERTAIN REGIONS OR SITUATIONS (part 3)

items.subtopic: IMMUNIZATIONS FOR CERTAIN REGIONS OR SITUATIONS (part 3)
items.title: Chikungunya Vaccine Considerations
items.content: A live chikungunya vaccine approved in the U.S. for individuals aged 18 and older at increased exposure risk is administered as a single dose. It is contraindicated in immunocompromised persons, with ~12% of recipients experiencing mild to moderate symptoms.
keyPoints: Chikungunya virus causes acute febrile illness with potential chronic arthralgias.
The vaccine is a single-dose regimen approved for those 18 and older at increased risk.
~12% of vaccinated individuals develop chikungunya-like symptoms, with 1.6% experiencing significant reactions.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · IMMUNIZATIONS FOR CERTAIN REGIONS OR SITUATIONS (part 3)

items.subtopic: MALARIA PREVENTION
items.title: Importance of Chemoprophylaxis in Malaria Prevention
items.content: Chemoprophylaxis is crucial for travelers to high-risk malaria regions. It involves taking antimalarial drugs before, during, and after travel to prevent symptomatic disease.
keyPoints: In 2018, nearly all of the 1823 malaria cases reported in the U.S. were acquired during travel to endemic countries.
Most cases occurred due to lack of chemoprophylaxis or inconsistent adherence.
Current drugs for prophylaxis include atovaquone-proguanil, chloroquine, doxycycline, mefloquine, primaquine, and tafenoquine.
Prophylaxis recommendations are based on travel itinerary, malaria risk, species prevalence, and drug resistance patterns.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · MALARIA PREVENTION

items.subtopic: MALARIA PREVENTION
items.title: Mosquito Avoidance Strategies for Malaria Prevention
items.content: Preventing mosquito bites is a key strategy in reducing the risk of malaria. This includes avoiding exposure during peak biting hours and using protective measures.
keyPoints: Anopheles mosquitoes, which transmit Plasmodium species, typically bite from dusk until dawn.
Travelers should wear long-sleeved shirts and pants, apply DEET-based repellents (20–50%), and use permethrin-treated clothing.
Sleeping under insecticide-treated bed nets is recommended if sleeping areas are not well screened.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · MALARIA PREVENTION

items.subtopic: MALARIA PREVENTION
items.title: Early Recognition of Malaria Symptoms in Travelers
items.content: Prompt identification and treatment of malaria symptoms can prevent progression to severe disease. Travelers should seek medical evaluation if they develop fever after visiting malarious areas.
keyPoints: Severe malaria can progress rapidly, making early recognition crucial.
Febrile travelers should be evaluated during or after travel to endemic regions.
Carrying standby emergency therapy is not recommended by the CDC but accepted by some authorities for lower-risk areas.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · MALARIA PREVENTION

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF OTHER ARTHROPOD-BORNE INFECTIONS
items.title: Importance of Arthropod Bite Avoidance in Preventing Arboviral Infections
items.content: Arthropod-borne infections, including arboviruses like dengue, chikungunya, and Zika, are transmitted by various vectors such as Aedes mosquitoes, non-Aedes mosquitoes, ticks, and biting midges. Due to limited availability of vaccines for many arboviral infections, bite avoidance is crucial in preventing these diseases.
keyPoints: Arthropod-borne infections include dengue, chikungunya, Zika, Japanese encephalitis, Mayaro virus, o'nyong-nyong virus, tick-borne encephalitis, Powassan virus, Oropouche virus, leishmaniasis, filariasis, African trypanosomiasis, Chagas disease, rickettsial diseases, and Lyme disease.
Vaccines are available for yellow fever, Japanese encephalitis, tick-borne encephalitis, dengue, and chikungunya but not all arboviral infections.
Arthropod bite avoidance is essential due to limited vaccine availability and potential medical contraindications.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · PREVENTION OF OTHER ARTHROPOD-BORNE INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF OTHER ARTHROPOD-BORNE INFECTIONS
items.title: Strategies for Avoiding Arthropod Bites During Travel
items.content: To prevent arthropod-borne infections, travelers should adopt strategies to avoid bites. These include wearing protective clothing, performing daily self-inspections for ticks, and avoiding environments like mud or thatch housing in areas endemic for Chagas disease.
keyPoints: Arthropods may bite during the day and across various environments, unlike Anopheles mosquitoes which primarily bite at night.
Travelers should tuck pants into socks to prevent tick bites and perform daily self-inspections.
Avoid sleeping in mud or thatch housing in areas endemic for Chagas disease.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · PREVENTION OF OTHER ARTHROPOD-BORNE INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: GASTROINTESTINAL ILLNESS (part 1)
items.title: Prevalence and Causes of Travelers' Diarrhea
items.content: Travelers' diarrhea (TD) is common among travelers, affecting 30-70% depending on the destination and season. It primarily results from bacterial infections (~80%), with pathogens like Escherichia coli, Campylobacter, Shigella, and Salmonella being most prevalent.
keyPoints: Prevalence: 30–70% of travelers report TD
Majority (~80%) caused by bacteria
Common bacterial pathogens: Escherichia coli, Campylobacter, Shigella, Salmonella
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · GASTROINTESTINAL ILLNESS (part 1)

items.subtopic: GASTROINTESTINAL ILLNESS (part 1)
items.title: Prevention Strategies for Travelers' Diarrhea
items.content: TD prevention focuses on food and beverage choices and hand hygiene. Safe practices include consuming cooked foods, avoiding uncooked fruits and vegetables unless peeled by the traveler, and drinking bottled or purified water.
keyPoints: Food should be cooked and served hot
Avoid uncooked fruits/vegetables unless peelable by traveler
Drink bottled/purified water; avoid ice in beverages
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · GASTROINTESTINAL ILLNESS (part 1)

items.subtopic: GASTROINTESTINAL ILLNESS (part 1)
items.title: Self-Treatment and Management of Travelers' Diarrhea
items.content: TD is generally self-limited, resolving in 3-7 days for bacterial infections. Treatment focuses on fluid and electrolyte replacement, with oral rehydration solutions ideal for severe cases. Mild to moderate TD can be managed with antimotility agents like loperamide.
keyPoints: Resolution time: 3–7 days for bacterial infections
Severe cases require oral rehydration solution
Mild/moderate TD can use antimotility agents (e.g., loperamide)
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · GASTROINTESTINAL ILLNESS (part 1)

items.subtopic: GASTROINTESTINAL ILLNESS (part 2)
items.title: Antibiotic Use in Traveler's Diarrhea: Risks and Recommendations
items.content: Antibiotics can reduce the duration of traveler's diarrhea (TD) to 1–2 days, but their use carries risks such as adverse effects, drug interactions, and microbiota alterations. These changes may lead to conditions like C. difficile colitis or colonization with multidrug-resistant organisms. Routine self-treatment is advised only for severe TD due to these concerns.
keyPoints: Antibiotics can decrease TD duration to 1–2 days
Risks include adverse effects, drug interactions, and microbiota alterations
Potential consequences: C. difficile colitis, multidrug-resistant organisms
Routine self-treatment recommended only for severe TD
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · GASTROINTESTINAL ILLNESS (part 2)

items.subtopic: GASTROINTESTINAL ILLNESS (part 2)
items.title: Preferred Antibiotic Regimens and Considerations for Traveler's Diarrhea
items.content: For self-treatment of traveler's diarrhea, azithromycin is preferred at a dose of 500 mg daily for 3 days or a single 1000-mg dose. Rifaximin or rifamycin SV are alternatives, especially when drug interactions preclude other antibiotics. However, their efficacy against dysentery and resistance in Campylobacter species should be considered.
keyPoints: Preferred antibiotic: azithromycin (500 mg daily for 3 days or single 1000-mg dose)
Alternatives: Rifaximin or rifamycin SV
Considerations: Drug interactions, resistance in Campylobacter species
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · GASTROINTESTINAL ILLNESS (part 2)

items.subtopic: ACTIVITY-SPECIFIC INFECTION RISKS | VENOUS THROMBOEMBOLISM | ALTITUDE ILLNESS | MOTION SICKNESS
items.title: Preventing Activity-Specific Infections During Travel
items.content: Travelers should take precautions to avoid infections linked to specific activities. Direct contact with freshwater bodies can lead to leptospirosis and schistosomiasis, particularly in Africa, Asia, and South America. Activities such as diving in Lake Malawi or rafting on the Nile increase risk for schistosomiasis. Wearing appropriate footwear is crucial in tropical regions to prevent infections like Strongyloides stercoralis and hookworm, as well as snakebites. Avoiding animals can minimize bite risks.
keyPoints: Avoid freshwater bodies to reduce leptospirosis and schistosomiasis risk.
Schistosomiasis is endemic in Africa, Asia, South America; high-risk activities include diving in Lake Malawi and rafting on the Nile.
Use appropriate footwear in tropical regions to prevent Strongyloides stercoralis, hookworm infections, and snakebites.
Avoid animals to minimize bite risk.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · ACTIVITY-SPECIFIC INFECTION RISKS | VENOUS THROMBOEMBOLISM | ALTITUDE ILLNESS | MOTION SICKNESS

items.subtopic: ACTIVITY-SPECIFIC INFECTION RISKS | VENOUS THROMBOEMBOLISM | ALTITUDE ILLNESS | MOTION SICKNESS
items.title: Managing Motion Sickness in Travelers
items.content: Motion sickness can significantly affect travelers, with nonpharmacologic interventions being the first line of prevention. These include avoiding triggers, staying hydrated, resting adequately, and optimizing seating positions near windows. For those with a history of motion sickness, over-the-counter antihistamines like dimenhydrinate or prescription options such as cyclizine can be effective but may cause sedation. Transdermal scopolamine is an alternative for prolonged exposure to motion risk, though it has anticholinergic effects. Nonsedating antihistamines are less effective. Complementary approaches like acupressure bands and ginger might benefit some travelers.
keyPoints: Nonpharmacologic interventions: avoid triggers, stay hydrated, rest, optimize seating near windows.
Over-the-counter antihistamines (e.g., dimenhydrinate) and prescription options (e.g., cyclizine) can prevent motion sickness but may cause sedation.
Transdermal scopolamine is effective for prolonged exposure but has anticholinergic effects, especially in elderly travelers.
Nonsedating antihistamines are less effective; complementary approaches like acupressure bands and ginger might help.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · ACTIVITY-SPECIFIC INFECTION RISKS | VENOUS THROMBOEMBOLISM | ALTITUDE ILLNESS | MOTION SICKNESS

items.subtopic: JET LAG
items.title: Managing Jet Lag: Strategies to Align Circadian Rhythms with New Time Zones
items.content: Jet lag results from the misalignment of circadian rhythms due to travel across multiple time zones. Symptoms include poor sleep, daytime sleepiness, gastrointestinal issues, and mood changes, particularly when crossing more than three time zones.
keyPoints: Symptoms are significant with travel across more than three time zones.
Strategies for adjustment: shift sleep schedules before travel, timed light exposure after arrival, melatonin use.
Caffeine can reduce daytime drowsiness but may disrupt sleep.
Prescription hypnotics like zolpidem should generally be avoided due to adverse effects such as excessive fatigue and impaired cognition.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · JET LAG | INJURIES

items.subtopic: INJURIES
items.title: Preventing Injuries During International Travel: Key Risks and Safety Measures
items.content: International travel increases the risk of injuries due to unfamiliar environments, language barriers, reliance on others for safety, and less stringent enforcement of safety measures in low- and middle-income countries. Increased risk-taking behaviors, often associated with alcohol use, further elevate injury risks.
keyPoints: Motor vehicle accidents are a common cause of injury deaths among travelers.
Risks include poor road conditions, lax traffic rule enforcement, riding motorbikes without helmets, overcrowded public transit, and vehicles lacking seatbelts or maintenance.
Drowning prevention and crime avoidance are crucial safety topics.
The U.S. State Department provides country-specific safety advice for U.S. travelers.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · JET LAG | INJURIES

items.subtopic: TRAVELERS WITH PREEEXISTING MEDICAL CONDITIONS | IMMUNOCOMPROMISED TRAVELERS
items.title: Managing Cardiovascular Risks for Travelers with Chronic Conditions
items.content: Travelers with chronic medical conditions, particularly cardiovascular diseases, face heightened risks during travel. In-flight emergencies and deaths often result from cardiovascular events. Changes in time zones and diet can disrupt the management of conditions like diabetes mellitus that require strict dietary control and medication timing.
keyPoints: Cardiovascular events are a frequent cause of in-flight emergencies and death during travel.
Travel across time zones and changes in diet can challenge conditions requiring regulation, such as diabetes mellitus.
Providers should provide copies of prescriptions, medical problem lists, and baseline electrocardiograms to assist travelers.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · TRAVELERS WITH PREEEXISTING MEDICAL CONDITIONS | IMMUNOCOMPROMISED TRAVELERS

items.subtopic: TRAVELERS WITH PREEEXISTING MEDICAL CONDITIONS | IMMUNOCOMPROMISED TRAVELERS
items.title: Vaccine Considerations for Immunocompromised Travelers
items.content: Immunocompromised travelers, including those with conditions like HIV/AIDS, organ transplants, and cancer, face increased infection risks and decreased vaccine efficacy. Live virus vaccines are contraindicated in severe immunosuppression cases, such as when CD4+ counts are <200 cells/μL or during high-dose glucocorticoid treatment.
keyPoints: Increased susceptibility to infections and decreased vaccine efficacy are concerns for immunocompromised travelers.
Live virus vaccines are contraindicated with severe immunosuppression, e.g., CD4+ count <200 cells/μL or ≥20 mg of prednisone daily for ≥2 weeks.
Travel vaccinations should be administered prior to iatrogenic immunosuppression (≥2 weeks for inactivated vaccines and ≥4 weeks for live vaccines).
Medical waivers may be necessary for travelers unable to receive yellow fever vaccine due to immunosuppression.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · TRAVELERS WITH PREEEXISTING MEDICAL CONDITIONS | IMMUNOCOMPROMISED TRAVELERS

items.subtopic: HIV-INFECTED TRAVELERS
items.title: Vaccination and Travel Guidelines for HIV-Infected Individuals
items.content: The risk of infection in HIV-infected travelers correlates with their level of immunosuppression, specifically the CD4+ T-cell count. Those with a CD4+ count >500 cells/μL face similar risks as non-immunosuppressed travelers. Live vaccines like MMR and varicella can be administered if the CD4+ count is >200 cells/μL for ≥6 months. Oral live attenuated typhoid and influenza vaccines are contraindicated due to safer alternatives.
keyPoints: CD4+ counts >500 cells/μL indicate similar travel risk as non-immunosuppressed individuals.
Live MMR and varicella vaccines can be given with CD4+ count >200 cells/μL for ≥6 months.
Oral live attenuated typhoid and influenza vaccines should not be used; prefer polysaccharide and inactivated versions respectively.
Entry restrictions on HIV-infected travelers have decreased, but policies vary by country.

items.subtopic: PREGNANT TRAVELERS
items.title: Travel Considerations for Pregnant Women
items.content: Pregnant travelers face unique risks such as limited emergency care, increased infection risk, and potential pregnancy complications from specific infections. Travel is safest between weeks 14–28 of pregnancy. Malaria prophylaxis options are limited to mefloquine and chloroquine due to drug resistance. Safe travel immunizations during pregnancy are also restricted.
keyPoints: Travel safest during weeks 14–28 of pregnancy, minimizing risks like morning sickness and impaired mobility.
Increased risk of infections such as malaria, influenza, hepatitis E, listeriosis, Zika virus, toxoplasmosis, and rubella.
Malaria prophylaxis options in pregnancy are limited to mefloquine and chloroquine.
DEET-based insect repellents are considered safe during pregnancy.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · HIV-INFECTED TRAVELERS | PREGNANT TRAVELERS

items.subtopic: TRAVELERS WITH SEVERE ALLERGIES | TRAVEL HEALTH KITS
items.title: Emergency Planning and Supplies for Travelers with Severe Allergies
items.content: Travelers with severe allergies should have a comprehensive emergency care plan, including carrying adequate supplies of self-treatment medications. This is crucial due to the increased risk of allergen exposure in unfamiliar environments and potential lack of immediate access to healthcare.
keyPoints: Carry epinephrine auto-injectors and antihistamines for emergency self-treatment.
Consider additional medications like rescue bronchodilator inhalers and short courses of glucocorticoids if no immediate healthcare is available.
Written documentation of allergic disorders and medications should be carried, especially for injectable medications.
Alert restaurants and hosts about severe food allergies; use printable allergy alert cards in various languages.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · TRAVELERS WITH SEVERE ALLERGIES | TRAVEL HEALTH KITS

items.subtopic: TRAVEL HEALTH KITS
items.title: Essential Components of a Travel Health Kit
items.content: A well-prepared travel health kit can reduce the need for medical care during travel by providing necessary medications and first-aid supplies. The contents should be tailored to the destination, duration, activities, and individual health needs.
keyPoints: Include routine and trip-specific prescription medications in original labeled bottles.

Carry over-the-counter medications such as analgesics, antipyretics, antidiarrheal agents, motion sickness remedies, antacids, laxatives, oral rehydration salts, antihistamines, and topical steroid creams.
Include basic first-aid items like gloves, bandages, tape, antibiotic ointment, and tweezers.
Critical medications should be in carry-on luggage to avoid loss or damage; consider restrictions on sharp objects or liquids.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · TRAVELERS WITH SEVERE ALLERGIES | TRAVEL HEALTH KITS

items.subtopic: TRAVELING WITH PRESCRIPTION MEDICATIONS | HEALTH CARE OVERSEAS AND TRAVEL HEALTH INSURANCE
items.title: Navigating International Travel with Prescription Medications
items.content: Travelers should carry copies of prescriptions or a signed medication list when traveling internationally, especially to regions like Asia, the Middle East, and Africa where drug restrictions are stringent. This includes controlled substances such as opioid analgesics, anxiolytics, sedatives, ADHD medications, and even some OTC drugs like pseudoephedrine and diphenhydramine. Advance approval from destination countries' health ministries may be required for restricted medications.
keyPoints: Carry copies of prescriptions or a signed medication list.
Countries in Asia, the Middle East, and Africa have stringent drug restrictions.
Controlled substances include opioid analgesics, anxiolytics, sedatives, ADHD medications.
OTC drugs like pseudoephedrine and diphenhydramine may be restricted.
Advance approval from health ministries might be necessary for certain medications.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · TRAVELING WITH PRESCRIPTION MEDICATIONS | HEALTH CARE OVERSEAS AND TRAVEL HEALTH INSURANCE

items.subtopic: TRAVELING WITH PRESCRIPTION MEDICATIONS | HEALTH CARE OVERSEAS AND TRAVEL HEALTH INSURANCE
items.title: Ensuring Safe and Adequate Health Care Coverage While Traveling Abroad
items.content: Travelers should review their health insurance policies before international travel to understand the scope of coverage, as most countries do not accept routine health insurance from other nations. Supplemental travel health insurance is advised for covering overseas healthcare costs, including emergency care, hospitalization, and medical evacuation if necessary.
keyPoints: Most countries do not accept routine foreign health insurance.
Review health insurance policies to assess international coverage scope.
Supplemental travel health insurance covers overseas healthcare costs.
Includes 24-h support centers and may cover medevac for inadequate local care.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · TRAVELING WITH PRESCRIPTION MEDICATIONS | HEALTH CARE OVERSEAS AND TRAVEL HEALTH INSURANCE

items.subtopic: SPECIAL TRAVEL POPULATIONS | POSTTRAVEL MEDICAL CARE | OUTBREAKS AND EMERGING INFECTIOUS DISEASES
items.title: Increased Risks for VFR Travelers and the Importance of Pretravel Advice
items.content: Visiting Friends and Relatives (VFR) travelers often face increased risks for travel-related infections due to their unique travel patterns, such as staying in local homes and adopting local habits. Many assume immunity against diseases like malaria based on previous exposure, which is incorrect. Barriers to receiving appropriate pretravel advice include financial constraints, language barriers, and mistrust of the medical system.
keyPoints: VFR travelers face increased risks for travel-related infections due to unique travel patterns.
Immunity from past malaria infection is not long-term; many VFR travelers incorrectly assume immunity.
Barriers to pretravel advice include financial issues, language barriers, and lack of trust in the medical system.
items.reference: Health Recommendations for International Travel · SPECIAL TRAVEL POPULATIONS | POSTTRAVEL MEDICAL CARE | OUTBREAKS AND EMERGING INFECTIOUS DISEASES

items.subtopic: SPECIAL TRAVEL POPULATIONS | POSTTRAVEL MEDICAL CARE | OUTBREAKS AND EMERGING INFECTIOUS DISEASES
items.title: Critical Considerations for Posttravel Medical Care and Emerging Infectious Diseases
items.content: Acute febrile illnesses in returning travelers can be life-threatening, necessitating early diagnosis and treatment. Infections like malaria, typhoid fever, leptospirosis, and acute schistosomiasis may have prolonged incubation periods up to a year, especially for malaria. Travelers should inform healthcare providers of their travel history even if illness onset is delayed. During outbreaks of novel or emerging infections, such as COVID-19 or Zika, information can rapidly change, affecting travel plans significantly.
keyPoints: Acute febrile illnesses in travelers can be life-threatening; early diagnosis and treatment are critical.
Infections like malaria, typhoid fever, leptospirosis, and acute schistosomiasis may have incubation periods up to a year.
Healthcare providers should consider travel history even if illness onset is delayed by up to a year for certain infections.
Emerging infectious diseases can rapidly change information and disrupt travel plans.
